title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about their musical tastes and experiences. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: In Tune: Four People on Music and Entertainment

texts.A:
### Isabelle, 32, Journalist
[43]I played the violin for twelve years as a child and teenager, reaching a fairly high level before university life made it impossible to continue.[/43] I still regret giving it up. [47]Music is deeply tied to my memory – certain songs can instantly transport me back to specific moments in my past with extraordinary clarity.[/47] I make playlists for every phase of my life as a kind of personal archive. [51]I find it almost impossible to work in silence – I always have music on, though it has to be instrumental, because lyrics distract me from writing.[/51] My colleagues find this strange but I can't function without background music.
texts.B:
### Kevin, 17, School Student
[44]I started making my own music last year using a laptop and free software, and it's become the most important thing in my life.[/44] I produce electronic tracks and post them online, and a few have actually got thousands of plays, which still surprises me. [48]What I love most about making music electronically is that you don't need expensive instruments or a recording studio – just a computer and an internet connection.[/48] [52]My parents think I should focus on my exams rather than spending so much time on music, and we argue about it regularly.[/52] But I honestly think music is where my future is.
texts.C:
### Diane, 61, Retired Civil Servant
I've been attending live concerts for over forty years and there's nothing else like it. [45]The thing about a live performance that no recording can replicate is the energy between the audience and the performer – you can feel it physically.[/45] I saw some extraordinary performances in the seventies and eighties when the music scene felt genuinely revolutionary. [49]I spend quite a lot of money on concert tickets each year and my husband thinks I'm extravagant, but experiences matter more to me than possessions.[/49] [53]I've noticed that younger audiences at concerts tend to spend more time filming on their phones than actually listening, and I find that terribly sad.[/53]
texts.D:
### Marcus, 38, Dentist
[46]I sing in an amateur choir that meets every Wednesday evening, and it's become the social highlight of my week.[/46] I'm not a particularly talented singer, but that doesn't matter – choir is genuinely for everyone. [50]Research suggests that singing in a group has remarkable benefits for mental health, and I can personally confirm this is true.[/50] After a stressful day at the surgery, an hour of singing with friends transforms my mood entirely. [54]We perform two or three public concerts a year, and the nervousness I feel before going on stage is something I've never quite managed to overcome despite years of performing.[/54]

questions.question: used to play a musical instrument seriously but had to stop?
questions.explanation: Isabelle played the violin for twelve years but had to give it up when university life made it impossible to continue.

questions.question: has recently started creating original music using a computer?
questions.explanation: Kevin started making his own electronic music using a laptop and free software last year.

questions.question: describes an aspect of live music that cannot be reproduced by listening to recordings?
questions.explanation: Diane says the energy between audience and performer at a live concert can be felt physically and no recording can replicate it.

questions.question: participates in a regular group musical activity that is also important to them socially?
questions.explanation: Marcus sings in an amateur choir every Wednesday, calling it the social highlight of his week.

questions.question: finds that music is strongly connected to their personal memories?
questions.explanation: Isabelle says certain songs can instantly transport her back to specific moments in her past with great clarity.

questions.question: points out that modern technology has made it possible to create music without traditional resources?
questions.explanation: Kevin says making electronic music only requires a computer and an internet connection, not expensive instruments or a studio.

questions.question: spends a significant amount on a music-related activity despite their partner's disapproval?
questions.explanation: Diane spends a lot on concert tickets each year and her husband thinks she is extravagant.

questions.question: mentions scientific evidence to support the positive effects of their musical activity?
questions.explanation: Marcus refers to research suggesting that singing in a group has remarkable benefits for mental health.

questions.question: needs to have music playing in order to concentrate on their work?
questions.explanation: Isabelle says she almost cannot work in silence and always has instrumental music on when writing.

questions.question: has experienced conflict with family members over the time they devote to music?
questions.explanation: Kevin says his parents want him to focus on exams rather than music and they argue about it regularly.
